title: Expanding democracy
description: Test your skills on how democracy expanded in the period from 1800 to 1848.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The apprehended danger from the experiment of universal suffrage applied to the whole legislative department is no dream of the imagination. .  . . The tendency of universal suffrage is to jeopardize the rights of property and the principles of liberty. There is a constant tendency . . . in the poor to covet and to share the plunder of the rich; in the debtor to relax or avoid the obligation of contracts; in the majority to tyrannize over the minority and trample down their rights in the indolent and and the profligate to cast the whole burdens of society upon the industrious and virtuous . . . . We are no longer to remain plain and simple republics of farmers. . . . We are fast becoming a great nation, with great commerce, manufactures, population, wealth, luxuries, and with the vices and miseries that they engender.”

>-James Kent, excerpt from the Proceedings and Debates of the Convention Assembled for the Purpose of Amending the Constitution of the State of New York, 1821

**Based on the text, which of the following statements would the author most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Extending suffrage to all white men would undermine the government and slow America’s progress.
Extending suffrage to all white men was the moral and ethical thing to do as the nation continued to expand.
Suffrage should extend to white women as they were responsible for the Republican education of their sons.
Suffrage should extend to all white men and all free black men as they were eligible for elected positions.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The apprehended danger from the experiment of universal suffrage applied to the whole legislative department is no dream of the imagination. .  . . The tendency of universal suffrage is to jeopardize the rights of property and the principles of liberty. There is a constant tendency . . . in the poor to covet and to share the plunder of the rich; in the debtor to relax or avoid the obligation of contracts; in the majority to tyrannize over the minority and trample down their rights in the indolent and and the profligate to cast the whole burdens of society upon the industrious and virtuous . . . . We are no longer to remain plain and simple republics of farmers. . . . We are fast becoming a great nation, with great commerce, manufactures, population, wealth, luxuries, and with the vices and miseries that they engender.”

>-James Kent, excerpt from the Proceedings and Debates of the Convention Assembled for the Purpose of Amending the Constitution of the State of New York, 1821

**The excerpt was designed to address which key concern?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: demands for the extension of the right to vote to all white men
requests for the end to the practice of enslaving people of African descent
appeals for the introduction of a secret ballot for all elections
calls for the extension of the right to vote to white women

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The apprehended danger from the experiment of universal suffrage applied to the whole legislative department is no dream of the imagination. .  . . The tendency of universal suffrage is to jeopardize the rights of property and the principles of liberty. There is a constant tendency . . . in the poor to covet and to share the plunder of the rich; in the debtor to relax or avoid the obligation of contracts; in the majority to tyrannize over the minority and trample down their rights in the indolent and the profligate to cast the whole burdens of society upon the industrious and virtuous . . . . We are no longer to remain plain and simple republics of farmers. . . . We are fast becoming a great nation, with great commerce, manufactures, population, wealth, luxuries, and with the vices and miseries that they engender.”

>-James Kent, excerpt from the Proceedings and Debates of the Convention Assembled for the Purpose of Amending the Constitution of the State of New York, 1821

**Which of the following groups would be most likely to support the views expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Whigs
Jacksonian Democrats
Progressives
Abolitionists

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Now as it would be palpably unjust and an act of great violence for one half of the number mentioned to deprive the other of their right of suffrage in the conformation of the original compact, so it would be proportionately unjust in any other given number. If ninety nine hundredths should combine to deprive the hundredth person of his right of elective franchise, tho' the crime would differ in degree of turpitude, it would nevertheless be of the same kind. This necessarily follows from the principle already advanced and explained, viz. that the right of suffrage cannot belong to one man without belonging to another; that it cannot belong to a part without belonging to the whole; and the whole being made up of parts, it follows that equal parts must have equal rights.”

>-James Cheetham, *A dissertation concerning political equality, and the Corporation of New-York,* University of Michigan, 1800

**Ideas similar to those expressed in the passage have directly contributed to which of the following developments?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: extension of suffrage to all white men
end of the practice of enslavement of people of African descent
use of the Australian secret ballot during elections
creation of a two-party political system

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Now as it would be palpably unjust and an act of great violence for one half of the number mentioned to deprive the other of their right of suffrage in the conformation of the original compact, so it would be proportionately unjust in any other given number. If ninety nine hundredths should combine to deprive the hundredth person of his right of elective franchise, tho' the crime would differ in degree of turpitude, it would nevertheless be of the same kind. This necessarily follows from the principle already advanced and explained, viz. that the right of suffrage cannot belong to one man without belonging to another; that it cannot belong to a part without belonging to the whole; and the whole being made up of parts, it follows that equal parts must have equal rights.”

>-James Cheetham, *A dissertation concerning political equality, and the Corporation of New-York,* University of Michigan, 1800

**The author’s remarks in the excerpt most directly reflected which of the following developments during the early nineteenth century?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the nation’s transition to a more participatory democracy
the rise in the belief that the United States has the God-given right to expand west
the growing debates and sectional differences over the extension of slavery to territories
the nation’s transition to a more manufacture-based economy

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Now as it would be palpably unjust and an act of great violence for one half of the number mentioned to deprive the other of their right of suffrage in the conformation of the original compact, so it would be proportionately unjust in any other given number. If ninety nine hundredths should combine to deprive the hundredth person of his right of elective franchise, tho' the crime would differ in degree of turpitude, it would nevertheless be of the same kind. This necessarily follows from the principle already advanced and explained, viz. that the right of suffrage cannot belong to one man without belonging to another; that it cannot belong to a part without belonging to the whole; and the whole being made up of parts, it follows that equal parts must have equal rights.”

>-James Cheetham, *A dissertation concerning political equality, and the Corporation of New-York,* University of Michigan, 1800

**The excerpt most strongly suggests that which of the following was correct in 1800?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Only white men with property had the right to vote.
All white men had the right to vote.
All white men and free black men had the right to vote.
Only married white men and their wives had the right to vote.

assessment_items.question_content:
>  “The question before us is the right of suffrage— who shall or who shall not have the right to vote. The committee have presented the scheme they thought best; to abolish all existing distinctions, and make the right of voting uniform. Is this not right? . . . The principle of the scheme now proposed is, that those who bear the burthens [sic] of the state, should choose those that rule it. — There is no privilege given to property, as such; but those who contribute to the public support, we consider as entitled to a share in the election of rulers.”

>-Nathan Sanford, excerpt from the [Report of the Debates and Proceedings of the Convention of the State of New York](https://books.google.com/books?id=n586AAAAcAAJ&dq), 1821

**The ideas expressed in the excerpt contributed most directly to which of the following?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: extension of the right to vote to all white males
expansion of citizenship to formerly enslaved persons
spread in the belief that there is an inherent goodness of people and nature
creation of new tariffs for manufactured goods

The committee have presented the scheme they thought best; to abolish all existing distinctions, and make the right of voting uniform. Is this not right? . . . >-Nathan Sanford, excerpt from the [Report of the Debates and Proceedings of the Convention of the State of New York](https://books.google.com/books?id=n586AAAAcAAJ&dq), 1821

**Which of the following movements expressed ideas most similar to the ideas expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: women’s rights movement
abolition movement
transcendentalism
nativism

The committee have presented the scheme they thought best; to abolish all existing distinctions, and make the right of voting uniform. Is this not right? . . . The principle of the scheme now proposed is, that those who bear the burthens [sic] of the state, should choose those that rule it. — There is no privilege given to property, as such; but those who contribute to the public support, we consider as entitled to a share in the election of rulers.”

**Arguments similar to those expressed in the excerpt were later employed to justify which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: extension of suffrage to white women
end to the National Bank of the United States
prohibition of the sale and consumption of alcohol
creation of an income tax amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
> “By the middle of the nineteenth century, thus, the nation had taken significant steps in the direction of universal white male suffrage. Spurred by the development of the economy, shifts in the social structure, the dynamics of party politics, the diffusion of democratic ideals, the experiences of war, and the need to maintain militias, the states, the federal government, and municipalities all had dismantled the most fundamental obstacles to the participation of men in elections. . . 
> “The expansion of the suffrage in fact played a key role in the enormous upsurge of political participation in the 1830s and 1840s, when turnout in some locales reached 80 percent of all adult male citizens.” 

>-Source: Alexander Keyssar, “The Right to Vote: the Contested History of Democracy in the United States,” 2009

**According to the passage, which of the following best explains the most important effect that the extension of suffrage to all white men  had on the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: More people voted in all levels of elections.
Fewer people voted in midterm elections than presidential elections.
Fewer people who owned property ran for political office.
There was more corruption at all levels of government.

**Which of the following pieces of evidence from 1820 to 1850 would best support the author’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: data showing the percentage of the voting eligible population that voted in presidential elections
data showing population growth in Western territories
data showing the change in how many electoral votes are allocated to each state
data showing the average socioeconomic status of white men

assessment_items.question_content:
> “By the middle of the nineteenth century, thus, the nation had taken significant steps in the direction of universal white male suffrage. Spurred by the development of the economy, shifts in the social structure, the dynamics of party politics, the diffusion of democratic ideals, the experiences of war, and the need to maintain militias, the states, the federal government, and municipalities all had dismantled the most fundamental obstacles to the participation of men in elections. . .

**The author’s claim that there was an “enormous upsurge of political participation” most directly contributed to which of the following characteristics of United States politics during the period?**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a transition to a participatory democracy
a shift towards a three-party political system
a realignment of political parties along regional lines
a transition to an elite democracy
